items.name: Eisenhower Matrix
items.description: Categorizes tasks by urgency and importance into 4 quadrants
items.best_for: Decision-making about what to work on
items.example: A production bug is urgent + important (do first). Learning Rust is important but not urgent (schedule time).

items.name: Time Blocking
items.description: Assigns specific time periods to specific tasks on your calendar
items.example: Block 9-11 AM every day for deep coding. Batch all meetings into 2-4 PM.

items.name: Getting Things Done (GTD)
items.description: Captures everything in an inbox, then organizes by context, energy, and time available
items.best_for: People with many projects and contexts (freelancers, tech leads)
items.complexity: High — requires inbox processing and weekly reviews
items.example: Capture 'fix API bug' in inbox, then organize to 'Office/High Energy' list for later.

items.name: Pomodoro Technique
items.description: Work in 25-minute focused intervals with 5-minute breaks
items.complexity: Very simple — just a timer and discipline
items.example: Set timer for 25 min, code without checking Slack. After 4 pomodoros, take a 20 min break.

items.name: Eat the Frog
items.description: Do your hardest, most dreaded task first thing in the morning
items.example: Write that documentation you've been avoiding before checking email.

comparisons.insight: No single framework works for everyone. Most productive developers combine 2-3 methods: Eisenhower Matrix for prioritization, Time Blocking for scheduling, and Pomodoro for execution. Experiment and keep what works for your brain.

keyInsight: Time management isn't about doing more — it's about doing the right things. The Eisenhower Matrix helps you decide WHAT, time blocking decides WHEN, and Pomodoro helps you EXECUTE. Layer these together for a complete system.
